standards.title: Access Control Policy
standards.description: Organizations shall establish, document, and enforce an access control policy based on business and information security requirements. The policy shall define rules for granting, reviewing, and revoking access to information and information processing facilities. Access control rules shall follow the principle of least privilege and need-to-know, ensuring users only receive the minimum access necessary for their role. The policy shall address both logical and physical access, cover all stages of the user lifecycle (onboarding, role change, offboarding), and be reviewed at planned intervals to ensure continued relevance.

standards.title: Identity Management
standards.description: The full lifecycle of identities shall be managed, including the creation, verification, assignment, and eventual removal of digital identities. Each identity shall be unique and attributable to a single entity (person, device, or service). The organization shall implement a formal registration and deregistration process for granting and revoking access. Identity verification shall be commensurate with the sensitivity of the systems or data being accessed. Shared or group identities shall be permitted only where business or operational requirements make individual identities impractical, and additional accountability controls shall be implemented in such cases.

standards.title: Authentication Information
standards.description: The allocation and management of authentication information (passwords, tokens, certificates, biometric data) shall be controlled through a formal management process. Default authentication information provided by vendors shall be changed upon installation. Temporary authentication information shall be unique to the individual and shall be forced to change on first use. Authentication information shall not be stored in clear text or easily reversible form. Users shall be required to follow good practices in the selection and use of authentication information, including keeping it confidential, not sharing it, and reporting suspected compromise immediately.

standards.title: Access Rights
standards.description: Access rights shall be provisioned, reviewed, modified, and removed in accordance with the organization's topic-specific policy on and rules for access control. Access rights shall be granted based on formal authorization from the owner of the information asset or system. A formal access provisioning process shall exist that maps user roles to required system access. Access rights shall be reviewed at regular intervals (at least annually for normal accounts, more frequently for privileged accounts) by asset owners. Access rights shall be adjusted or removed promptly upon change of role, termination, or contract completion. The principle of least privilege shall be applied throughout the access rights lifecycle.

standards.title: Privileged Access Rights
standards.description: The allocation and use of privileged access rights shall be restricted and managed. Privileged access rights shall be allocated on a need-to-use basis, aligned with the access control policy, and granted only to users who have been specifically authorized. Privileged access shall be assigned to a user identity separate from the one used for routine activities. The competence of users with privileged access shall be verified before granting elevated rights. The use of generic administration accounts shall be avoided, and if used, shared secret credentials shall be changed frequently. Privileged access rights shall be reviewed more frequently than normal access rights (at minimum quarterly) and removed promptly when no longer required.

standards.title: Information Access Restriction
standards.description: Access to information and other associated assets shall be restricted in accordance with the established topic-specific policy on access control. Access restrictions shall be applied through technical mechanisms such as access control lists, role-based access control, or attribute-based access control. The system shall enforce access restrictions at all access points, including application interfaces, APIs, direct database access, and file system access. Sensitive information shall be isolated from general-purpose systems where appropriate. Access restrictions shall be tested periodically to verify they are effective and cannot be bypassed through alternative access paths.

standards.title: Access to Source Code
standards.description: Read and write access to source code, development tools, and software libraries shall be appropriately managed. Access to source code shall be restricted based on roles and responsibilities within the development team. Write access to production source code repositories shall require additional authorization and be limited to approved developers. Code review and approval processes shall be enforced before changes are merged to production branches. Access to source code repositories shall be logged, and access rights shall be reviewed when personnel change roles or leave the development team. Source code containing sensitive algorithms, security mechanisms, or cryptographic implementations shall receive additional access protection.

standards.title: Secure Authentication
standards.description: Secure authentication technologies and procedures shall be established and implemented based on information access restrictions and the topic-specific policy on access control. Authentication mechanisms shall be appropriate to the sensitivity of the information being accessed. Multi-factor authentication shall be used for access to critical systems, remote access, and privileged accounts. Authentication systems shall protect credentials during transmission using encryption and shall store credentials using strong, irreversible hashing algorithms. Login processes shall limit the information disclosed upon authentication failure (e.g., not revealing whether the username or password was incorrect). Authentication events shall be logged for security monitoring and incident investigation.

standards.title: Inventory of Information and Other Associated Assets
standards.description: An inventory of information and other associated assets, including their owners, shall be identified and maintained. In the context of IAM, this includes maintaining an accurate inventory of all identity stores (Active Directory, LDAP, cloud identity providers), service accounts, API keys, certificates, and other authentication credentials. The inventory shall identify the owner responsible for each identity asset, the systems that depend on it, and its lifecycle status. Without a complete inventory of identity assets, organizations cannot effectively manage access, detect orphaned accounts, or respond to identity-related security incidents.

standards.title: Acceptable Use of Information and Other Associated Assets
standards.description: Rules for the acceptable use of information and other associated assets shall be identified, documented, and implemented. In the IAM context, acceptable use policies shall cover the use of organizational accounts (prohibiting sharing of credentials, requirement to use assigned accounts), acceptable authentication methods, rules for password/passphrase selection, requirements for screen locking and session management, and consequences of policy violations. Users shall acknowledge their understanding and acceptance of acceptable use policies. The policy shall be communicated to all users including contractors, temporary staff, and third parties with system access.

standards.title: Segregation of Duties
standards.description: Conflicting duties and conflicting areas of responsibility shall be segregated to reduce opportunities for unauthorized or unintentional modification or misuse of organizational assets. In the IAM context, the person who requests access shall not be the same person who approves it. The person who administers identity systems shall not be the same person who audits them. The person who creates user accounts shall not be the same person who assigns privileges. Organizations shall identify duties that require segregation through risk assessment and implement technical controls (such as workflow-enforced approvals and RBAC with mutually exclusive roles) to prevent any single individual from performing conflicting functions.

standards.title: Information Security Incident Management Planning and Preparation
standards.description: The organization shall plan and prepare for managing information security incidents by establishing incident management processes and responsibilities. In the IAM context, this includes preparing for identity-related incidents such as credential compromise, unauthorized access, privilege escalation, identity system outages, and mass account lockouts. Incident response plans shall include procedures for emergency credential reset, emergency account disabling, identity provider failover, and communication with affected users. Playbooks shall be developed for common IAM incidents such as password spray attacks, phishing campaigns, and compromised service accounts.

standards.title: Logging
standards.description: Logs that record activities, exceptions, faults, and other relevant events shall be produced, stored, protected, and analysed. In the IAM context, this includes logging all authentication events (successful and failed), authorization decisions, account lifecycle events (creation, modification, deletion), privilege changes, password resets, MFA enrollment changes, and federated authentication flows. Logs shall include sufficient detail to support forensic investigation, including timestamps, source IP addresses, user agents, and session identifiers. Log integrity shall be protected through write-once storage, log signing, or forwarding to a tamper-evident centralized log management system.

standards.title: Monitoring Activities
standards.description: Networks, systems, and applications shall be monitored for anomalous behaviour and appropriate actions taken to evaluate potential information security incidents. For IAM, monitoring shall include detecting brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks, identifying unusual login patterns (time, location, device), detecting privilege escalation attempts, monitoring for use of dormant or disabled accounts, and identifying lateral movement patterns. The organization shall establish baselines for normal identity-related activity and configure alerts for significant deviations. Security information and event management (SIEM) systems should correlate identity events with other security telemetry to detect sophisticated attacks.

standards.title: Use of Cryptography (Identity-Related Aspects)
standards.description: Rules for the effective use of cryptography, including cryptographic key management, shall be defined and implemented. In the IAM context, this covers the use of cryptography for credential storage (password hashing algorithms such as Argon2, bcrypt, scrypt), TLS for authentication traffic, certificate-based authentication, token signing and encryption (JWT, SAML assertions), and cryptographic authenticators (FIDO2 keys). Key management procedures shall address key generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. Cryptographic algorithms and key lengths shall be selected based on current industry standards and regulatory requirements, and shall be reviewed periodically to ensure continued adequacy against advancing cryptanalytic capabilities.

standards.title: Secure Development Lifecycle (Identity-Related Aspects)
standards.description: Rules for the secure development of software and systems shall be established and applied. In the IAM context, secure development practices shall include implementing authentication and authorization as cross-cutting concerns using established frameworks rather than custom code, conducting security reviews of authentication flows, performing threat modeling of identity components using STRIDE methodology, implementing automated security testing of authentication endpoints (fuzzing, injection testing), and following secure coding standards for credential handling (never logging credentials, using parameterized queries for identity stores). Development teams shall receive training on common identity-related vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10, CWE identity weaknesses).

standards.title: Information Security in Supplier Relationships
standards.description: Processes and procedures shall be defined and implemented to manage the information security risks associated with the use of supplier products or services. For IAM, this includes assessing the identity and access management practices of suppliers who have access to organizational systems, requiring suppliers to use the organization's identity provider where possible (federated access), defining minimum authentication requirements for supplier access (multi-factor authentication), restricting supplier access to the minimum necessary resources and time periods, and monitoring supplier access activity. Supplier access shall be reviewed regularly and removed promptly when no longer required.

standards.title: Addressing Information Security Within Supplier Agreements
standards.description: Relevant information security requirements shall be established and agreed upon with each supplier based on the type of supplier relationship. IAM-specific contractual requirements shall include the supplier's obligation to enforce multi-factor authentication for all access to organizational systems, password and credential management standards the supplier must follow, incident notification requirements for identity compromise events, the organization's right to audit supplier identity and access management practices, data retention and deletion requirements for identity data processed by the supplier, and requirements for the supplier to maintain and provide access logs. These contractual protections ensure suppliers maintain IAM standards consistent with the organization's own policies.

standards.title: Screening
standards.description: Background verification checks on all candidates to become personnel shall be carried out prior to joining the organization, on an ongoing basis, and shall take into account applicable laws, regulations, and ethics. In the IAM context, screening supports the identity proofing process by verifying that the individual is who they claim to be before provisioning access credentials. The level of screening shall be proportionate to the sensitivity of the role — personnel who will have privileged access or access to sensitive systems shall undergo more thorough screening. Screening results shall inform the initial access rights assigned and any restrictions placed on the account.

standards.title: Terms and Conditions of Employment
standards.description: The employment contractual agreements shall state the personnel's and the organization's responsibilities for information security. IAM-related terms shall include the requirement to protect authentication credentials, the obligation to use only authorized accounts and access methods, acknowledgment of monitoring and logging of access activities, the requirement to report suspected security incidents including credential compromise, and the consequences of policy violations. These contractual obligations provide the legal foundation for enforcing IAM policies and enable the organization to take action when users violate access control requirements.

standards.title: Responsibilities After Termination or Change of Employment
standards.description: Information security responsibilities and duties that remain valid after termination or change of employment shall be defined, enforced, and communicated. For IAM, this requires that all access rights be promptly reviewed and adjusted upon role change, and that all access be revoked promptly upon termination. Return of all physical authenticators (tokens, smart cards, keys) shall be required. Exit interviews shall include reminders of ongoing confidentiality obligations. The organization shall have procedures to ensure that departure information reaches account administrators in a timely manner so that accounts can be disabled on or before the last working day. Non-disclosure agreements relating to access to sensitive systems shall survive termination.

standards.title: Physical Entry Controls
standards.description: Secure areas shall be protected by appropriate entry controls to ensure that only authorized personnel are allowed access. While primarily a physical security control, physical entry controls have significant IAM implications. Physical access control systems shall integrate with the organization's identity management infrastructure where possible, using the same identity store for both logical and physical access. Badge-based access shall be tied to individual identity records and shall be automatically disabled when an employee's logical access is revoked. Physical access logs shall be correlated with logical access logs to detect anomalies such as a user authenticating to a system while their badge shows they are not in the building.

standards.title: User Endpoint Devices
standards.description: Information stored on, processed by, or accessible via user endpoint devices shall be protected. In the IAM context, endpoint device security directly impacts authentication assurance because compromised devices can be used to steal credentials, intercept authentication tokens, and bypass multi-factor authentication. Device management policies shall require device encryption, up-to-date security patches, endpoint detection and response (EDR), and screen lock after inactivity. The organization shall implement device compliance checks as part of the access decision process — devices that do not meet minimum security requirements shall be denied access or granted limited access. This is a core zero trust concept: device posture informs access decisions.

standards.title: Policies for Information Security
standards.description: A set of policies for information security shall be defined, approved by management, published, communicated to and acknowledged by relevant personnel and relevant interested parties, and reviewed at planned intervals or if significant changes occur. The information security policy set shall include an IAM-specific policy or IAM sections within the overarching security policy. The IAM policy shall define the organization's approach to identity management, authentication standards, access control models, privileged access management, federation, and compliance requirements. Senior management approval of the IAM policy demonstrates organizational commitment and provides the mandate for implementing and enforcing IAM controls across the organization.

standards.title: Information Transfer
standards.description: Information transfer rules, procedures, or agreements shall be in place for all types of transfer facilities within the organization and between the organization and other parties. For IAM, this includes the secure transfer of identity data between systems during provisioning (e.g., SCIM protocol), the exchange of authentication assertions during federation (SAML, OIDC), the transfer of audit logs to centralized security monitoring systems, and the exchange of identity information during mergers, acquisitions, or organizational restructuring. All identity data in transit shall be protected with appropriate encryption. Transfer agreements shall specify the format, security requirements, and responsibilities of each party.

standards.title: Configuration Management
standards.description: Configurations, including security configurations, of hardware, software, services, and networks shall be established, documented, implemented, monitored, and reviewed. For IAM systems specifically, configuration management is critical because misconfigured identity providers, directory services, or federation endpoints can create significant security vulnerabilities. Configuration management shall cover identity provider settings (session timeouts, MFA policies, password policies), federation trust configurations, API gateway authentication settings, RBAC/ABAC policy definitions, and directory service schema and replication settings. Configuration changes to IAM systems shall be subject to change management processes with security review and rollback capability.

standards.title: Compliance with Policies, Rules and Standards for Information Security
standards.description: Compliance with the organization's information security policy, topic-specific policies, rules, and standards shall be regularly reviewed. For IAM, compliance reviews shall verify that authentication standards are being met (MFA adoption rate, password policy compliance), access reviews are being conducted on schedule, privileged accounts comply with separation requirements, account lifecycle processes are functioning (timely deprovisioning, no orphaned accounts), and federation configurations meet security standards. Non-compliance shall be reported to management and remediated within defined timeframes. Regular IAM compliance reviews provide assurance that security controls remain effective and identify drift from established policies before they can be exploited.

standards.title: Contact with Special Interest Groups
standards.description: The organization shall establish and maintain contact with special interest groups or other specialist security forums and professional associations. For IAM professionals, this includes participation in identity-focused organizations such as the Identity Defined Security Alliance (IDSA), the FIDO Alliance, the OpenID Foundation, and national identity management working groups. Participation enables the organization to stay current with emerging identity threats, new authentication technologies, evolving standards and best practices, and industry-specific identity requirements. This intelligence feeds into the organization's IAM strategy and helps ensure that identity controls keep pace with the evolving threat landscape.

standards.title: Networks Security
standards.description: Networks and network devices shall be secured, managed, and controlled to protect information in systems and applications. In the IAM context, network security directly supports identity controls through network segmentation that isolates identity infrastructure (directory servers, certificate authorities, federation endpoints) from general-purpose networks. Network-level authentication (802.1X) shall be implemented to ensure that only authenticated devices can connect to the network. Identity-aware network access control (NAC) integrates device identity with user identity to make network access decisions. Zero trust network architecture eliminates implicit trust based on network location and requires authentication and authorization for every network connection.
